name: Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with your feet firmly on the floor.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists straight.
Unrack the bar and lower it slowly to your mid-chest.
Press the bar back up explosively until arms are fully extended.
Keep your shoulder blades retracted and back slightly arched throughout.
tips: Drive your feet into the floor for full-body tension.
The bar path should be slightly diagonal, not straight up and down.
description: A fundamental compound pressing movement performed lying on a flat bench. Primarily targets the pectorals with significant tricep and shoulder involvement.
commonMistakes: Flaring the elbows out to 90 degrees, which stresses the shoulder joint.
Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of controlling the descent.
Losing leg drive and arch, which destabilizes the entire lift.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
instructions: Set a bench to a 30–45 degree incline.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at chest level, palms facing forward.
Press the dumbbells up and together until arms are fully extended.
Lower them slowly back to the starting position.
Keep your shoulder blades pinched together throughout.
tips: A 30-degree incline focuses more on the upper chest than 45 degrees.
Avoid letting the dumbbells drift too wide at the bottom.
description: A compound pressing exercise on an inclined bench using dumbbells. Emphasizes the upper chest (clavicular head) more than flat pressing.
commonMistakes: Setting the bench too steep (above 45 degrees) which shifts emphasis to front delts.
Allowing the dumbbells to drift too wide at the bottom of the movement.
Losing scapular retraction midway through the set.

name: Cable Fly
instructions: Set the cables to shoulder height and attach D-handles.
Stand in the center of the cable machine with a staggered stance.
With a slight bend in the elbows, bring your hands together in front of you.
Slowly open your arms back to the starting position feeling the chest stretch.
Maintain constant tension — do not let the weight stack touch.
tips: Imagine hugging a large tree to get the right movement pattern.
The lower the cable position, the more upper chest activation.
description: An isolation chest exercise using cable pulleys set at shoulder height. Provides constant tension throughout the full arc of motion.
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and bending the elbows excessively, turning it into a press.
Allowing the weight stack to touch between reps, losing tension.
Not maintaining a slight bend in the elbow throughout.

name: Chest Dips
instructions: Grip parallel bars and support your body with arms extended.
Lean your torso slightly forward (15–30 degrees) to target the chest.
Lower your body by bending your elbows until you feel a chest stretch.
Press back up to the starting position.
Add weight via a dip belt for progressive overload.
tips: Leaning forward increases chest activation; upright increases tricep focus.
Do not bounce at the bottom — control the descent.
description: A bodyweight compound movement performed on parallel bars with a forward lean. Effectively targets the lower and outer chest fibers.
commonMistakes: Staying too upright, which shifts focus from chest to triceps.
Bouncing at the bottom of the rep instead of controlling the stretch.
Going too deep and straining the shoulder joint.

name: Push-Ups
instructions: Start in a plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows.
Push through your palms to return to the starting position.
Keep your core braced throughout the movement.
tips: For more chest activation, use a wider hand position.
Elevate your feet to increase difficulty and upper chest emphasis.
description: A classic bodyweight pressing exercise performed on the floor. Develops the chest, triceps, and shoulders while also engaging the core.
commonMistakes: Letting the hips sag, turning the movement into a back-bending exercise.
Placing hands too wide or too narrow for comfort.
Only performing partial reps without touching the chest to the floor.

name: Decline Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Set the bench to a 15–30 degree decline.
Secure your feet under the ankle pads.
Grip the barbell at shoulder-width and unrack.
Lower the bar to your lower chest.
Press the bar back up to the starting position.
tips: The decline press targets the lower pectoral fibers.
Use a spotter — getting pinned on a decline bench is dangerous.
description: A barbell press performed on a decline bench to target the lower pectoral fibers. Reduces shoulder strain compared to flat pressing for some lifters.
commonMistakes: Not using a spotter when getting pinned is especially dangerous on a decline.
Gripping too wide and flaring the elbows excessively.
Rushing through the eccentric phase without control.

name: Pec Deck Machine
instructions: Sit in the machine with your back flat against the pad.
Place your forearms against the pads with elbows at 90 degrees.
Bring the pads together in a hugging motion.
Slowly return to the starting position, feeling the chest stretch.
Do not let the weight stack touch between reps.
tips: Adjust the seat so that the pads are at chest level.
Focus on squeezing the chest at the peak contraction.
description: A machine isolation exercise that mimics the fly motion with forearm pads. Provides a strong mid-range and peak contraction for the chest.
commonMistakes: Positioning the elbows too high, which can cause shoulder impingement.
Using momentum to swing the pads together.
Not adjusting the seat height so pads are at chest level.

name: Dumbbell Pullover
instructions: Lie perpendicular to a bench with your upper back supported.
Hold a single dumbbell with both hands directly over your chest.
Lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head, keeping elbows slightly bent.
Feel the stretch across your chest and lats.
Pull the dumbbell back to the starting position.
tips: Keep your hips low to maximize the stretch.
This exercise works both the chest and lats — adjust focus with torso angle.
description: A unique exercise where a dumbbell is lowered behind the head while lying on a bench. Engages both the chest and lats depending on torso angle.
commonMistakes: Bending the elbows too much, reducing the stretch and making it a tricep exercise.
Raising the hips during the movement, reducing the stretch.
Using too much weight before mastering the movement.

name: Barbell Row
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and hinge at the hips until torso is near-parallel.
Grip the barbell slightly wider than shoulder-width, overhand grip.
Drive your elbows back to pull the bar to your lower ribcage.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner back to the starting position.
tips: Keep your lower back flat — do not round under load.
Pull to the lower abdomen for more lat activation; upper abdomen for more upper back.
description: A heavy compound rowing movement performed in a hinged torso position. One of the best exercises for overall back thickness and lat width.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back under heavy load.
Using too much torso swing to lift the bar.
Pulling the bar to the upper chest instead of the lower abdomen.

name: Pull-Up
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with an overhand grip, slightly wider than shoulders.
Engage your core and depress your shoulder blades.
Pull your body up until your chin clears the bar.
Lower yourself with control to a full hang.
Avoid using momentum or kipping.
tips: Think of pulling your elbows down to your hips for maximum lat activation.
Add weight via a dip belt or weight vest for progressive overload.
description: A demanding bodyweight compound pulling movement on a bar. Builds the lats, biceps, and upper back effectively.
commonMistakes: Using a kipping motion to complete reps with poor form.
Not achieving full arm extension at the bottom of each rep.
Pulling with the biceps rather than initiating with the lats.

name: Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown machine with your thighs secured under the pad.
Grip the bar wider than shoulder-width with an overhand grip.
Pull the bar down to your upper chest, leaning back slightly.
Squeeze your lats at the bottom of the movement.
Slowly extend your arms to return to the starting position.
tips: Avoid pulling the bar behind your neck — it strains the cervical spine.
Focus on driving your elbows to your hips, not pulling with your arms.
description: A cable-machine pulling exercise that mimics the pull-up motion. An excellent alternative for those who cannot yet perform pull-ups.
commonMistakes: Pulling the bar behind the neck, straining the cervical spine.
Leaning back excessively and turning it into a row.
Not fully extending the arms at the top of the movement.

name: Seated Cable Row
instructions: Sit at a cable row machine with feet on the platform, knees slightly bent.
Grab the close-grip handle with a neutral grip.
Sit up straight and pull the handle toward your lower abdomen.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the peak contraction.
Slowly extend your arms while keeping your torso upright.
tips: Avoid rounding your lower back — keep a neutral spine.
Do not lean back excessively; use your back muscles, not momentum.
description: A compound rowing exercise using a cable machine while seated. Targets the mid-back, lats, and biceps with constant tension.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back during the row.
Leaning back excessively to generate momentum.
Not fully extending the arms between reps.

name: T-Bar Row
instructions: Load one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or a corner.
Straddle the bar and grip the handles near the loaded end.
Hinge at the hips to a near-parallel torso position.
Pull the bar to your chest, driving elbows back.
Lower with control and repeat.
tips: Use a V-handle attachment to allow full range of motion.
Keep your chest up and back flat to prevent injury.
description: A compound rowing exercise using one end of a loaded barbell. Allows heavy loading and targets the entire back musculature.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back under heavy load.
Jerking the weight up using momentum.
Not achieving a full squeeze of the shoulder blades at the top.

name: Conventional Deadlift
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, bar over your mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and grip the bar just outside your legs.
Take a deep breath, brace your core, and flatten your back.
Push the floor away with your legs while keeping the bar close to your body.
Stand tall, hips and shoulders rising at the same rate.
tips: Think 'push the floor away' rather than 'pull the bar up'.
Keep the bar in contact with your shins and thighs throughout the lift.
description: The foundational barbell pulling movement from the floor. Works the entire posterior chain including back, glutes, and hamstrings.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back, especially during the initial pull from the floor.
Letting the bar drift away from the body during the pull.
Jerking the bar from the floor instead of applying steady pressure.

name: Face Pull
instructions: Set a cable pulley to head height and attach a rope.
Grip each end of the rope and step back from the machine.
Pull the rope toward your face, separating your hands at the end.
Your elbows should be at or above shoulder height.
Squeeze the rear deltoids and external rotators at peak contraction.
tips: An excellent exercise for rear delt and rotator cuff health.
High reps (15–20) work well for this isolation exercise.
description: A cable exercise pulling a rope to the face with elbows high. Excellent for rear delt and rotator cuff health and development.
commonMistakes: Setting the cable too low so elbows drop below shoulder height.
Using too much weight and pulling with the biceps instead of rear delts.
Not separating the rope ends at the peak contraction.

name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
instructions: Place one knee and hand on a bench for support.
Hold a dumbbell in the opposite hand, arm fully extended.
Pull the dumbbell to your hip, driving your elbow back.
Squeeze the lat at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbell with control to the starting position.
tips: Allow the shoulder blade to protract fully at the bottom for full range of motion.
Keep your hips and shoulders square to the bench.
description: A unilateral rowing movement performed with one hand and knee supported on a bench. Allows a full range of motion for each side independently.
commonMistakes: Rotating the hips to use momentum rather than isolating the back.
Not allowing the shoulder blade to fully protract at the bottom.
Pulling the dumbbell to the hip by bending the elbow instead of driving it back.

name: Barbell Overhead Press
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, grip the bar just outside shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar and hold it at chin level.
Press the bar straight overhead, tucking your chin slightly.
Once the bar passes your head, push your head through the 'window' of your arms.
Lower the bar back to chin height under control.
tips: Keep your core braced and glutes squeezed — avoid hyperextending the lower back.
Slightly tilt the bar path back to vertical by moving your head forward.
description: A standing barbell press overhead that builds shoulder strength and mass. Also engages the triceps and upper traps significantly.
commonMistakes: Hyperextending the lower back instead of keeping a neutral spine.
Pressing straight up instead of slightly back behind the head.
Not locking out the arms fully overhead.

name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing your body.
With a slight bend at the elbow, raise the dumbbells to your sides.
Lift until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Hold briefly at the top, then lower with control.
Lead with your pinkies to maximize medial delt activation.
tips: Avoid using momentum by swinging your torso.
Slightly tilting the dumbbell (pinky higher than thumb) increases lateral head activation.
description: An isolation movement raising dumbbells out to the sides. The primary exercise for developing the medial (lateral) deltoid head.
commonMistakes: Swinging the torso to generate momentum.
Raising the dumbbells too high above parallel, shifting to traps.
Bending the elbows too much, reducing lateral delt tension.

name: Dumbbell Front Raise
instructions: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand, palms facing down.
Raise both dumbbells in front of you to shoulder height.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout.
Lower with control to the starting position.
Can be done alternating or simultaneously.
tips: Avoid swinging — keep movements slow and controlled.
Use lighter weight than lateral raises as the anterior delt is stronger.
description: An isolation exercise raising dumbbells forward to shoulder height. Primarily targets the anterior (front) deltoid head.
commonMistakes: Swinging the body to create momentum.
Raising the dumbbells above shoulder height unnecessarily.
Using too heavy a weight and compromising form.

name: Reverse Dumbbell Fly
instructions: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend at the knees.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging down.
With a slight bend in the elbows, raise the dumbbells out to your sides.
Squeeze your shoulder blades and rear delts at the top.
Lower the dumbbells with control.
tips: Keep the movement in the plane of your back, not in front of you.
Can also be done on a rear-delt machine or with cables.
description: A bent-over isolation exercise raising dumbbells laterally. Effectively targets the posterior deltoids and upper back.
commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of maintaining a flat torso.
Swinging the arms rather than using controlled lateral raises.
Moving arms in front of the torso plane instead of in line with the back.

name: Arnold Press
instructions: Sit on a bench, holding dumbbells at shoulder height with palms facing you.
Rotate your palms outward as you press the dumbbells overhead.
At the top, palms should be facing forward with arms fully extended.
Reverse the rotation as you lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Maintain a controlled tempo throughout.
tips: The rotating motion of the Arnold Press hits all three delt heads.
Do not go too heavy — control matters more than load here.
description: A dumbbell shoulder press invented by Arnold Schwarzenegger with a rotating palm motion. Uniquely hits all three deltoid heads through its full range.
commonMistakes: Rushing through the rotation and skipping the full range of motion.
Using too much weight and losing the rotational control.
Flaring the elbows excessively at the bottom.

name: Barbell Upright Row
instructions: Stand with a barbell in front of you, overhand grip shoulder-width or closer.
Pull the bar up along your body until it reaches chin level.
Lead with your elbows — they should be above your hands at the top.
Lower the bar with control.
Keep the bar close to your body throughout.
tips: Use a wider grip to reduce impingement risk.
Do not pull the bar behind the vertical plane of your shoulders.
description: A compound pulling movement raising a barbell to chin level with elbows high. Works the lateral delts and traps simultaneously.
commonMistakes: Using a grip too narrow, increasing shoulder impingement risk.
Raising the elbows above the bar height at the top.
Using excessive body swing to initiate the pull.

name: Cable Lateral Raise
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the lowest position.
Stand sideways to the cable machine and grab the handle with the far hand.
Raise the cable out to your side until your arm is parallel to the floor.
Lower with control, maintaining tension throughout.
Cable provides constant tension unlike dumbbells.
tips: Cross your arm slightly in front of your body at the start for a better line of pull.
Keep a slight bend in the elbow throughout.
description: A lateral raise variation using a low cable pulley for constant tension. Provides resistance throughout the full range unlike dumbbells.
commonMistakes: Leaning too far away from the machine to use heavier weight.
Bending the elbow too much and turning it into a row.
Not maintaining consistent tension throughout the set.

name: Barbell Shrug
instructions: Stand with a barbell in front of you at arm's length.
Shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears.
Hold for 1 second at the top.
Lower the shoulders with control.
Avoid rolling the shoulders — only vertical movement.
tips: Heavy weight and moderate reps (8–12) build thick traps.
Use wrist straps if grip becomes the limiting factor.
description: A simple trap isolation exercise shrugging a barbell upward. The primary movement for building upper trap thickness and size.
commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders forward or backward instead of straight up.
Using too much weight with partial range of motion.
Not pausing briefly at the top of the shrug.

name: Barbell Curl
instructions: Stand with a barbell using an underhand grip, shoulder-width apart.
Keep your upper arms stationary against your torso.
Curl the bar up by contracting your biceps.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze.
Lower with control back to the starting position.
tips: Avoid swinging your body — keep tension on the biceps.
A straight bar emphasizes the long head; an EZ-bar reduces wrist strain.
description: The classic standing curl with a barbell using an underhand grip. A fundamental bicep mass builder allowing heavy loading.
commonMistakes: Swinging the body to assist the curl.
Letting the upper arms drift forward at the top of the movement.
Using a grip that is too wide, reducing bicep activation.

name: Dumbbell Curl
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand, arms at your sides.
With palms facing forward, curl both dumbbells up simultaneously.
Alternatively, alternate arms for greater focus per rep.
Pause at the top and squeeze the bicep.
Lower with control.
tips: Supinate (rotate) your wrists as you curl for maximum bicep contraction.
Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout.
description: A bilateral or alternating curl using dumbbells with supination. Allows independent arm movement and a stronger peak contraction.
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbow to drift forward at the top, reducing tension.
Not supinating the wrist during the curl.
Swinging both dumbbells simultaneously using body momentum.

name: Hammer Curl
instructions: Stand with dumbbells in each hand, palms facing your body (neutral grip).
Keeping your upper arm stationary, curl the dumbbells up.
The neutral grip works the brachialis and brachioradialis.
Pause at the top, then lower with control.
Can be done alternating or simultaneously.
tips: Hammer curls build the brachialis which pushes the bicep peak higher.
Cable rope hammer curls provide constant tension throughout.
description: A curl using a neutral (hammer) grip targeting the brachialis and brachioradialis. Builds arm thickness and the bicep peak.
commonMistakes: Rotating the wrists during the curl, defeating the neutral grip purpose.
Swinging the arms to generate momentum.
Not fully extending the arms at the bottom between reps.

name: Preacher Curl
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench with your upper arms resting on the pad.
Grip an EZ-bar at shoulder width with an underhand grip.
Curl the bar upward toward your chin.
Squeeze at the top without losing contact with the pad.
Lower slowly — the eccentric is especially effective here.
tips: The preacher bench removes shoulder involvement for strict isolation.
Do not lock out fully — keep tension on the bicep at the bottom.
description: A strict curl performed with upper arms braced on a preacher bench. Eliminates body swing for maximum bicep isolation.
commonMistakes: Locking out the arms fully, which reduces tension on the bicep.
Bouncing the weight off the pad at the bottom.
Gripping too wide or narrow, causing wrist discomfort.

name: Cable Curl
instructions: Set the cable pulley to the lowest position.
Attach a straight bar or EZ-bar attachment.
Stand facing the machine and curl the bar up toward your shoulders.
Squeeze the bicep at the top.
Lower with control, feeling constant tension from the cable.
tips: Cables keep tension on the muscle throughout the full range of motion.
Face away from the machine for an overhead cable curl variation.
description: A bicep curl using a low cable pulley for constant tension. Particularly effective at the bottom of the range where dumbbells are weakest.
commonMistakes: Letting the elbows drift forward, reducing bicep tension.
Using too much body swing to assist.
Not maintaining elbow position throughout the movement.

name: Concentration Curl
instructions: Sit on a bench with legs spread and a dumbbell between your legs.
Rest your elbow on the inside of your thigh.
Curl the dumbbell up toward your shoulder.
Fully contract the bicep at the top.
Lower with control for maximum eccentric benefit.
tips: The concentrated position removes momentum and isolates the bicep.
Supinate your wrist at the top for maximum peak contraction.
description: A seated curl with the elbow braced against the inner thigh. Maximally isolates the bicep without any shoulder involvement.
commonMistakes: Moving the elbow away from the thigh during the curl.
Using momentum rather than slow controlled reps.
Stopping short of full contraction at the top.

name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
instructions: Set a bench to 45–60 degrees and sit back against it.
Let your arms hang straight down, holding dumbbells.
Curl the dumbbells up while keeping your upper arms vertical.
The incline position creates a stretch on the long head of the bicep.
Lower with full control to maximize the stretch.
tips: The pre-stretched position of the long head increases EMG activation.
Use lighter weight than standing curls due to the mechanical disadvantage.
description: A dumbbell curl performed on an inclined bench with arms hanging. The pre-stretched position of the long head increases activation significantly.
commonMistakes: Not allowing arms to hang completely straight at the start.
Using too heavy a weight and losing the pre-stretch benefit.
Swinging the upper arms away from vertical during the curl.

name: Spider Curl
instructions: Set a preacher bench backward, lying on the angled pad chest-down.
Let your arms hang straight down and grip an EZ-bar.
Curl the bar up toward your face.
Squeeze the biceps at the top.
Lower with control — full range of motion is key.
tips: Spider curls are particularly effective at creating the bicep peak.
Can also be done with dumbbells for unilateral training.
description: A curl performed lying chest-down on an angled bench. The position keeps constant tension on the biceps throughout the full range.
commonMistakes: Not using full range of motion from full extension.
Using momentum by bouncing the weight at the bottom.
Allowing the upper arms to drift from vertical during the curl.

name: Skull Crusher
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and hold an EZ-bar with arms extended over your chest.
Lower the bar toward your forehead by bending only at the elbows.
Your upper arms should remain vertical throughout.
Press the bar back up to the starting position.
Keep elbows from flaring out too wide.
tips: EZ-bar is more comfortable on the wrists than a straight bar.
Slightly bringing the bar behind your head increases long head activation.
description: A lying tricep isolation movement lowering a barbell toward the forehead. Excellent for long head development and overall tricep mass.
commonMistakes: Flaring the elbows outward, reducing tricep isolation.
Letting the upper arms drift backward during the movement.
Using too heavy a weight and losing control near the forehead.

name: Tricep Pushdown
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position with a straight bar or rope.
Stand facing the machine, grip the attachment with an overhand grip.
Keep your upper arms stationary at your sides.
Push the bar down until your arms are fully extended.
Squeeze the triceps at the bottom, then slowly return.
tips: Leaning forward slightly allows better range of motion.
Using a rope and spreading at the bottom increases lateral head activation.
description: A cable isolation exercise pushing down from a high pulley. The most common tricep exercise for cable-based isolation work.
commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms away from the body during the push.
Not fully extending the arms at the bottom.
Using too much weight and converting it into a lat pulldown motion.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell with a shoulder-width grip.
Unrack the bar and keep your elbows close to your body.
Lower the bar to your lower chest.
Press the bar back up explosively.
Keep elbows tucked — not flared — throughout the movement.
tips: Going too narrow can strain the wrists — stay shoulder-width.
This is one of the best compound tricep movements.
description: A barbell bench press with a shoulder-width grip emphasizing the triceps. One of the best compound tricep movements available.
commonMistakes: Gripping too narrow (inside shoulder width) which strains the wrists.
Flaring the elbows out rather than keeping them tucked.
Letting the bar drift too high toward the clavicle.

name: Overhead Tricep Extension
instructions: Sit or stand holding a single dumbbell with both hands over your head.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head by bending at the elbows.
Feel the stretch in the long head of the tricep.
Press the dumbbell back up to full extension.
Keep your elbows pointing straight up throughout.
tips: The overhead position stretches the long head more than any other tricep exercise.
Can also be done with a cable, EZ-bar, or kettlebell.
description: An overhead extension with a dumbbell held in both hands behind the head. The overhead position provides a superior stretch for the long head.
commonMistakes: Flaring the elbows outward during the movement.
Not lowering the weight far enough behind the head.
Arching the lower back excessively when seated.

name: Tricep Dips
instructions: Grip parallel bars with arms straight, keeping your torso upright.
Lower your body by bending your elbows, keeping them close to your sides.
Lower until your elbows reach 90 degrees or just below.
Press back up to full extension.
Keep your body more upright to emphasize triceps over chest.
tips: Lean upright (not forward) for more tricep isolation.
Add weight with a dip belt for progressive overload.
description: A bodyweight tricep movement performed on parallel bars with an upright torso. Allows progressive overload via added weight for serious tricep development.
commonMistakes: Leaning too far forward, shifting focus from triceps to chest.
Not reaching full elbow extension at the top.
Dipping too low and causing excessive shoulder strain.

name: Tricep Kickback
instructions: Hinge forward with a dumbbell in one hand, upper arm parallel to floor.
Keeping your upper arm stationary, extend your forearm back.
Squeeze the tricep when your arm is fully extended.
Slowly bring the dumbbell back to the starting position.
Repeat on both sides.
tips: The kickback only produces peak contraction in the fully extended position.
Use a cable for constant tension throughout the movement.
description: A hinge-based isolation exercise extending the forearm backward. Provides peak contraction at full extension but limited overall tension.
commonMistakes: Moving the upper arm during the exercise instead of keeping it parallel to the floor.
Using too heavy a weight and not achieving full extension.
Performing the movement too quickly without a squeeze at extension.

name: Diamond Push-Up
instructions: Start in a push-up position with hands placed together, forming a diamond with thumbs and forefingers.
Keep your body in a straight line.
Lower your chest to your hands while keeping elbows close to your body.
Push back up to the starting position.
Keep your core braced throughout.
tips: This variation significantly increases tricep activation vs. regular push-ups.
Elevate your feet for more difficulty.
description: A push-up variation with hands close together forming a diamond shape. Dramatically increases tricep activation compared to standard push-ups.
commonMistakes: Letting the elbows flare out wide instead of staying close to the body.
Allowing the hips to sag during the movement.
Placing hands too close together, straining the wrists.

name: JM Press
instructions: Lie on a bench and grip the barbell with a shoulder-width grip.
Lower the bar in a combined press and skull crusher motion.
The bar should land around your lower throat/upper chest.
Press back up along the same arc.
This is a hybrid between a close-grip bench and a skull crusher.
tips: Invented by powerlifter JM Blakley for massive tricep overload.
Keep the weight moderate — form is critical due to the unusual bar path.
description: A hybrid movement combining a close-grip bench press and skull crusher. Invented by powerlifter JM Blakley for maximum tricep overload.
commonMistakes: Using too much weight before learning the unusual bar path.
Letting the bar drift too far from the throat toward the chest.
Not maintaining elbow control throughout the arc.

name: Barbell Back Squat
instructions: Place the barbell on your upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar).
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly out.
Brace your core and begin the descent by pushing your knees out.
Lower until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up.
tips: Keep your chest up and avoid caving the knees inward.
Depth depends on your anatomy — squat as deep as you can with good form.
description: The foundational barbell squat with the bar on the upper or lower back. The king of lower body compound movements for quad and overall leg development.
commonMistakes: Caving the knees inward during the descent or ascent.
Shifting onto the toes and losing heel contact with the floor.
Rounding the lower back at the bottom of the squat.

name: Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand with a barbell at hip height, overhand grip shoulder-width.
Hinge at the hips and push them back, lowering the bar along your legs.
Lower until you feel a deep stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position.
Keep the bar close to your body throughout.
tips: RDL trains the hamstrings through the hip extension function.
Do not round your lower back — maintain a neutral spine.
description: A hip-hinge movement lowering the bar along the legs while keeping the knees mostly straight. The premier hamstring and glute exercise through hip extension.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back at the bottom of the movement.
Bending the knees too much, turning it into a conventional deadlift.
Letting the bar drift away from the legs during the hinge.

name: Leg Press
instructions: Sit in a leg press machine with feet shoulder-width on the platform.
Release the safety handles and lower the platform until knees reach 90 degrees.
Press the platform back up without locking out the knees fully.
Keep your back and tailbone flat against the pad throughout.
Foot placement higher on the platform targets glutes and hamstrings more.
tips: Never let your lower back round off the pad at the bottom.
Single-leg leg press is excellent for addressing imbalances.
description: A machine-based compound leg press pushing a weighted platform. Allows heavy leg loading without the spinal compression of barbell squats.
commonMistakes: Allowing the lower back to round off the pad at full depth.
Locking out the knees completely at the top.
Placing feet too low on the platform, increasing knee stress.

name: Lying Leg Curl
instructions: Lie face down on a leg curl machine with the pad just below your calves.
Curl your legs up as high as possible without lifting your hips.
Squeeze the hamstrings at the top.
Lower with control to the starting position.
Keep your hips pressed against the pad throughout.
tips: Pointing your toes slightly inward increases the short head activation.
Dorsiflexing the feet (toes toward shins) increases the stretch.
description: A lying machine exercise curling the heels toward the glutes. The primary isolation exercise for hamstring development.
commonMistakes: Lifting the hips off the pad to assist the curl.
Not achieving full range of motion from complete extension.
Letting the weight drop without controlling the eccentric phase.

name: Bulgarian Split Squat
instructions: Stand with your back to a bench, holding dumbbells at your sides.
Place one foot behind you on the bench.
Lower your back knee toward the floor, keeping the front shin vertical.
Drive through your front foot to return to the starting position.
Keep your torso upright throughout.
tips: Foot position forward increases glute activation; foot under hip increases quad focus.
Start without weight to master the balance.
description: A single-leg squat with the rear foot elevated on a bench. Highly effective for quad and glute development with a large range of motion.
commonMistakes: Placing the front foot too close to the bench, causing excessive forward knee travel.
Allowing the front knee to cave inward during the movement.
Not keeping the torso upright throughout the exercise.

name: Hack Squat
instructions: Load the hack squat machine and position your feet shoulder-width on the platform.
Release the safety handles and descend by bending at the knees.
Lower until thighs are parallel to the platform.
Press back up through your full foot.
Keep your back flat against the pad at all times.
tips: Hack squats allow a more quad-focused squat with less spinal loading.
A narrow foot position increases quad outer sweep activation.
description: A machine squat with a fixed backpad allowing a quad-focused movement. Reduces spinal loading while maintaining effective quad stimulus.
commonMistakes: Locking out the knees at the top, removing tension from the quads.
Allowing the lower back to round off the backpad.
Using too narrow a foot stance and overloading the knees.

name: Leg Extension
instructions: Sit in the leg extension machine with the pad across your shins.
Extend your legs until they are straight.
Squeeze the quadriceps at the top.
Lower with control — do not let the weight drop.
Can be done unilaterally to address imbalances.
tips: Full extension is important to recruit the rectus femoris.
Avoid using excessively heavy weight due to knee joint stress.
description: A seated machine exercise extending the legs from a bent to straight position. The primary isolation exercise for the quadriceps.
commonMistakes: Using excessively heavy weight that causes pain at the knee joint.
Not achieving full extension at the top of each rep.
Letting the weight drop rather than controlling the eccentric.

name: Good Morning
instructions: Place a barbell on your upper back as in a squat.
Stand with feet hip-width apart.
Hinge at the hips, lowering your torso forward until nearly parallel.
Keep a slight bend in the knees and a neutral spine.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing.
tips: Good mornings strengthen the posterior chain — hamstrings, glutes, and lower back.
Start light to master the hinge pattern before adding weight.
description: A barbell exercise hinging the torso forward with the bar on the back. Effectively trains the hamstrings, glutes, and lower back erectors.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back under load, which is dangerous.
Bending the knees too much, reducing hamstring involvement.
Loading too heavy before mastering the hip hinge pattern.

name: Standing Calf Raise
instructions: Position yourself on the standing calf raise machine with pads on your shoulders.
Stand with the balls of your feet on the platform, heels hanging off.
Rise up onto your toes as high as possible.
Hold briefly at the top.
Lower your heels below the platform level for a full stretch.
tips: The full range of motion (stretch at bottom, squeeze at top) is crucial.
Slow eccentrics (3–5 seconds down) are very effective for calf growth.
description: A machine exercise rising onto the toes from a standing position. The primary calf exercise for gastrocnemius development.
commonMistakes: Using partial range of motion without reaching full dorsiflexion stretch.
Bouncing at the bottom rather than controlling the stretch.
Rushing through the movement without pausing at the top.

name: Seated Calf Raise
instructions: Sit at the seated calf raise machine with pads on your lower thighs.
Place the balls of your feet on the platform.
Rise onto your toes as high as possible.
Hold at the top, then lower with control.
The seated position isolates the soleus more than the gastrocnemius.
tips: The soleus responds particularly well to higher reps (15–25).
A pause at the bottom stretch increases hypertrophy stimulus.
description: A seated machine calf raise targeting the soleus muscle. The seated position bends the knee, emphasizing the soleus over the gastrocnemius.
commonMistakes: Not using the full range of motion from full stretch to full contraction.
Using too much weight that prevents complete range.
Moving too quickly through reps without pausing at the stretch.

name: Donkey Calf Raise
instructions: Position yourself on the donkey calf raise machine leaning forward.
Place the balls of your feet on the platform.
Rise onto your toes as high as possible.
Lower your heels for a full stretch.
The bent-hip position pre-stretches the gastrocnemius.
tips: The donkey calf raise provides a greater gastrocnemius stretch due to the hip angle.
Can be improvised with a partner sitting on your lower back.
description: A calf raise performed bent at the hips to pre-stretch the gastrocnemius. Provides a greater range of motion than standing variations.
commonMistakes: Not lowering the heels enough to get a full stretch.
Using a hunched torso position that reduces effectiveness.
Bending the knees too much, reducing gastrocnemius involvement.

name: Single-Leg Calf Raise
instructions: Stand on one foot on the edge of a step.
Lower your heel below the step for a full calf stretch.
Rise up onto your toes as high as possible.
Perform all reps on one leg before switching.
Use a wall or railing for balance support if needed.
tips: Single-leg raises double the load per calf compared to bilateral.
A slow, full-range-of-motion rep is far more effective than a fast, partial one.
description: A unilateral calf raise on the edge of a step. Doubles the load per calf compared to bilateral variations for greater stimulus.
commonMistakes: Using partial range of motion instead of full stretch at the bottom.
Not holding at the top for a brief pause.
Moving too quickly and using bounce to complete reps.

name: Crunch
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
Place your hands behind your head or cross them over your chest.
Curl your upper body forward, lifting your shoulder blades off the ground.
Squeeze your abs at the top.
Lower with control — do not flop back down.
tips: Only raise your shoulder blades — do not sit up fully.
Exhale as you crunch to get a stronger ab contraction.
description: A classic abdominal exercise curling the upper torso toward the knees. Targets the rectus abdominis through spinal flexion.
commonMistakes: Pulling on the neck with the hands instead of supporting it lightly.
Using momentum to swing the upper body up.
Lifting the lower back off the floor and turning it into a sit-up.

name: Cable Crunch
instructions: Kneel in front of a cable machine with a rope attachment at the top.
Grip the rope and hold it at face level.
Crunch your torso down, rounding your lower back.
Pull your elbows toward your knees.
Return to the starting position with control.
tips: Cable crunches allow progressive overload for the abs unlike bodyweight crunches.
Hold the rope steady at your face — the movement should come only from your abs.
description: A kneeling crunch using a cable and rope attachment from above. The only abdominal crunch variant that allows true progressive overload.
commonMistakes: Moving the hands away from the face, turning it into a pull-down.
Rounding the hips rather than curling the torso.
Using too much weight and losing core control.

name: Plank
instructions: Start in a forearm plank position with elbows under your shoulders.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Brace your core, squeeze your glutes, and push through your forearms.
Breathe steadily and hold for the target duration.
Avoid letting your hips sag or rise.
tips: Quality beats duration — a 30-second perfect plank beats 2 minutes with sagging hips.
Add weight plates on your back for progressive overload.
description: A static isometric core exercise maintaining a straight body in a forearm support position. Builds core endurance and anti-extension strength.
commonMistakes: Letting the hips sag toward the floor.
Raising the hips too high into a pike position.
Holding breath instead of breathing steadily throughout.

name: Russian Twist
instructions: Sit on the floor with knees bent, leaning back slightly.
Clasp your hands together or hold a weight plate.
Rotate your torso to the left, touching the floor beside you.
Rotate to the right side.
Elevate your feet for more difficulty.
tips: The rotation comes from your obliques, not your arms.
Keep your spine neutral — avoid excessive rounding.
description: A seated rotational core exercise twisting the torso side to side. Primarily targets the obliques and transverse abdominis.
commonMistakes: Rounding the spine excessively during the rotation.
Using the arms to swing the weight rather than rotating from the core.
Performing the movement too quickly without controlled rotation.

name: Hanging Leg Raise
instructions: Hang from a pull-up bar with a shoulder-width overhand grip.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Raise your legs to horizontal (or higher for advanced).
Lower with control, avoiding swinging.
For more difficulty, keep legs straight rather than bending at the knees.
tips: Posterior pelvic tilt at the top ensures greater ab activation.
Avoid using momentum — let the abs do the work.
description: A hanging exercise raising the legs toward horizontal or beyond. Trains the lower abs and hip flexors through hip flexion.
commonMistakes: Using momentum and swinging the body to raise the legs.
Not tilting the pelvis posteriorly at the top for full ab contraction.
Bending the knees excessively to make the movement easier when advanced.

name: Ab Wheel Rollout
instructions: Kneel on the floor and hold the ab wheel handles.
Roll forward slowly while keeping your core braced.
Extend until your body is nearly parallel to the floor.
Use your abs to pull the wheel back toward your knees.
Do not let your lower back hyperextend.
tips: Start with partial rollouts if you cannot do a full rollout.
Posterior pelvic tilt (tuck pelvis) prevents lower back injury.
description: A kneeling exercise rolling an ab wheel forward and back. One of the most challenging and effective anti-extension core exercises.
commonMistakes: Hyperextending the lower back at full extension.
Not bracing the core before rolling out.
Rolling out further than current strength allows.

name: Dragon Flag
instructions: Lie on a bench and grip it behind your head.
Lift your entire body up into a vertical position supported only by your shoulders.
Lower your body as a rigid unit, keeping it straight.
Stop just before your body touches the bench.
Pull back up to the vertical position.
tips: This exercise popularized by Bruce Lee requires exceptional core strength.
Use negative-only dragon flags initially — lower very slowly, then step back up.
description: A full-body lever exercise raising and lowering the body as a rigid unit from the shoulders. An extremely advanced core strength movement.
commonMistakes: Arching or piking the body instead of remaining rigid.
Moving too fast through the eccentric phase.
Attempting this before building sufficient core strength.

name: Hollow Body Hold
instructions: Lie on your back and press your lower back firmly into the floor.
Raise your legs to about 45 degrees and arms overhead.
Maintain the posterior pelvic tilt (lower back pressed to floor).
Hold for the target duration.
For more difficulty, lower your legs toward the floor.
tips: The hollow body position is foundational for gymnastics and calisthenics.
If you cannot maintain lower back contact, raise your legs higher.
description: A gymnastic-based isometric hold maintaining a scooped body position. Foundational for gymnastics and an excellent anti-extension core exercise.
commonMistakes: Allowing the lower back to lift off the floor.
Holding breath instead of breathing during the hold.
Lowering the legs too far before the lower back lifts.

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench, a barbell across your hips.
Plant your feet flat, shoulder-width apart.
Drive your hips upward by squeezing your glutes.
Hold at the top for 1 second with full hip extension.
Lower with control.
tips: Use a barbell pad for comfort under the barbell.
Push through your heels, not your toes, for better glute activation.
description: A hip extension exercise with the upper back on a bench and a barbell across the hips. The most effective exercise for glute hypertrophy through hip extension.
commonMistakes: Not achieving full hip extension at the top of the movement.
Letting the hips drop too low before driving up again.
Placing the barbell too high on the hips causing discomfort.

name: Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent, feet flat on the floor.
Press through your heels to lift your hips toward the ceiling.
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top.
Lower your hips back down.
Add a barbell or dumbbell across your hips for resistance.
tips: The glute bridge is the foundation for hip thrust progression.
Keeping feet closer together increases glute max activation.
description: A floor-based hip extension exercise driving the hips toward the ceiling. A beginner-friendly progression toward the barbell hip thrust.
commonMistakes: Not squeezing the glutes at the top of the movement.
Pressing through the toes instead of the heels.
Allowing the lower back to arch excessively at the top.

name: Cable Glute Kickback
instructions: Attach an ankle strap to the lowest cable pulley.
Face the machine and hold onto it for support.
Kick your leg straight back, extending at the hip.
Squeeze your glute at the peak contraction.
Lower with control and repeat.
tips: Keep a slight bend in the standing knee.
Lean slightly forward to increase the glute activation range.
description: A cable-based isolation exercise extending one leg behind the body. Effectively isolates the gluteus maximus through hip extension.
commonMistakes: Kicking too high and hyperextending the lower back.
Using momentum rather than a controlled hip extension.
Not maintaining a slight bend in the standing knee.

name: Hip Abduction Machine
instructions: Sit in the hip abduction machine with pads on the outside of your knees.
Push your legs outward against the resistance.
Hold at the outer position for a moment.
Return to the starting position with control.
Works the gluteus medius and minimus.
tips: Lean slightly forward to shift emphasis to the gluteus medius.
High reps (15–25) work well for this muscle group.
description: A seated machine exercise pushing the legs outward against resistance. Targets the gluteus medius and minimus for hip stability.
commonMistakes: Using excessive momentum to slam the pads apart.
Not controlling the return phase of the movement.
Sitting too upright instead of leaning slightly forward.

name: Box Step-Up
instructions: Stand in front of a box or bench holding dumbbells.
Step one foot onto the box.
Drive through the heel of the raised foot to stand on the box.
Step back down and repeat on the same side or alternate.
Keep your torso upright throughout.
tips: A higher box increases the range of motion and glute activation.
Drive through the heel of the elevated leg, not the toe of the grounded leg.
description: A unilateral exercise stepping up onto a box or bench. Develops the glutes and quads with a functional single-leg movement pattern.
commonMistakes: Pushing off the grounded foot instead of driving through the elevated heel.
Leaning too far forward reducing glute activation.
Stepping down too quickly without controlling the descent.

name: Barbell Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a bench and hold a barbell with an underhand grip.
Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging off the knees.
Lower the bar by extending the wrists.
Curl the bar up by flexing the wrists.
Work through a full range of motion.
tips: High reps (15–25) work well for forearm development.
Also can be done on a preacher bench pad for more stability.
description: A seated isolation exercise curling the wrists upward with a barbell. Develops the wrist flexor muscles on the inner forearm.
commonMistakes: Using too heavy a weight that prevents full range of motion.
Not going through the complete range from full extension to full flexion.
Moving the forearms off the thighs during the exercise.

name: Reverse Wrist Curl
instructions: Sit on a bench and hold a barbell with an overhand grip.
Rest your forearms on your thighs, wrists hanging off the knees.
Lower the bar by flexing the wrists down.
Raise the bar by extending the wrists upward.
Targets the wrist extensors (back of forearm).
tips: Lighter weights than regular wrist curls — extensors are weaker than flexors.
Balanced training of both sides prevents wrist injury.
description: A seated isolation exercise extending the wrists upward with a barbell. Develops the wrist extensor muscles on the outer forearm.
commonMistakes: Using the same weight as regular wrist curls when extensors are weaker.
Not fully extending the wrists at the top of the movement.
Moving the forearms during the exercise.

name: Farmer's Walk
instructions: Hold a heavy dumbbell or kettlebell in each hand.
Stand tall with core braced and shoulder blades retracted.
Walk forward with short, controlled steps.
Keep the weights from swinging.
Walk for a set distance or time.
tips: One of the best exercises for overall strength and grip.
Single-arm carries (suitcase carry) challenge core stability asymmetrically.
description: A loaded carry exercise walking with heavy weights at the sides. Builds grip strength, trap endurance, and full-body stability simultaneously.
commonMistakes: Leaning to one side when carrying unilateral loads.
Rounding the shoulders forward instead of retracted.
Taking overly long strides that cause weight swinging.

name: Dead Hang
instructions: Grip a pull-up bar with an overhand grip.
Let your body hang freely with arms fully extended.
Relax your lower body and breathe.
Hold for the target duration.
Builds grip strength and decompresses the spine.
tips: Dead hangs are excellent for shoulder health and grip endurance.
Progress to one-arm hangs as you become stronger.
description: A passive hang from a pull-up bar with arms fully extended. Builds grip strength and endurance while decompressing the spine.
commonMistakes: Using a grip that is too wide, reducing grip endurance.
Not fully relaxing the shoulder girdle to get spinal decompression.
Rushing off the bar without completing the full intended duration.

name: Reverse Curl
instructions: Stand holding a barbell with an overhand grip shoulder-width apart.
Curl the bar up using the overhand grip.
Your brachioradialis (forearm) does more work than with underhand curls.
Lower with control.
Keep your elbows close to your body.
tips: The reverse curl directly targets the brachioradialis, the largest forearm muscle.
Use an EZ-bar to reduce wrist strain with the overhand grip.
description: A curl using an overhand grip targeting the brachioradialis and wrist extensors. Builds forearm thickness and balanced arm development.
commonMistakes: Letting the elbows drift forward at the top.
Using too much body swing to assist.
Not using full range of motion from complete extension.

name: Barbell Shrug
instructions: Stand with a barbell in front of you, overhand grip shoulder-width.
Shrug shoulders straight up toward your ears.
Hold at the top for 1 second.
Lower with control.
Avoid rolling the shoulders — vertical movement only.
tips: Heavy weight with moderate reps (8-12) builds thick traps.
Use straps if grip is limiting.
description: A vertical shrug movement with a barbell to build upper trap thickness. One of the most direct trap exercises available.
commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders in a circular motion instead of straight up.
Using so much weight that range of motion is compromised.
Not pausing briefly at the top of each rep.

name: Dumbbell Shrug
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Shrug your shoulders up toward your ears.
Hold at the top, then lower slowly.
Keep your arms straight throughout.
Do not tilt your neck.
tips: Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion than a barbell.
Go heavy and focus on the squeeze at the top.
description: A dumbbell shrug allowing slightly greater range of motion than barbell. Targets the upper trapezius for thickness and strength.
commonMistakes: Rotating the shoulders forward or backward instead of straight vertical.
Not pausing at the peak contraction.
Using momentum from the legs to initiate the shrug.

name: Cable Shrug
instructions: Stand between two cables set at the lowest position.
Grip one handle in each hand.
Shrug your shoulders upward.
Cable provides constant tension throughout the movement.
Lower with control.
tips: Cable version offers resistance at every point in the shrug.
Can also be done with a single cable from behind.
description: A shrug performed with cables providing constant tension throughout. Effective for upper trap isolation with variable resistance.
commonMistakes: Not maintaining an upright posture during the movement.
Pulling with the arms instead of pure shoulder elevation.
Not fully lowering the shoulders between reps.

name: Rack Pull
instructions: Set safety pins in a rack at knee height.
Stand with the barbell resting on the pins, feet hip-width apart.
Pull the bar from the pins using a conventional deadlift motion.
Stand fully tall, squeezing your traps at the top.
Lower back to the pins under control.
tips: Rack pulls allow heavier loads than deadlifts due to the shorter range.
Excellent for upper back and trap development.
description: A partial deadlift starting from knee height allowing heavier loads. Excellent for upper back, trap, and grip development.
commonMistakes: Rounding the upper back under very heavy loads.
Setting the pins too high and reducing the training benefit.
Not locking the hips out fully at the top.

name: Farmer's Carry (Traps Focus)
instructions: Hold heavy dumbbells or a trap bar at your sides.
Walk with short steps, keeping your shoulders retracted.
The loaded shrug of carrying builds trap endurance.
Walk for a target distance or time.
Keep your core braced throughout.
tips: Shoulder retraction (not elevation) during the carry targets upper traps.
Use the heaviest weight you can carry with good posture.
description: A loaded walk with heavy implements carried at the sides with shoulders retracted. Builds trap endurance and overall shoulder stability.
commonMistakes: Allowing the shoulders to roll forward during the walk.
Taking steps that are too large causing weight to swing.
Failing to maintain scapular retraction throughout.

name: Trap Bar Deadlift
instructions: Stand inside a trap (hex) bar with feet hip-width apart.
Grip the handles and hinge at the hips.
Brace your core and drive through your legs to stand.
Keep your chest up and back flat throughout.
Lower the bar back to the floor with control.
tips: The trap bar deadlift is more quad-dominant than a straight bar deadlift.
Excellent for athletes and beginners learning to deadlift safely.
description: A deadlift using a hex bar allowing a more upright torso position. More quad-dominant than straight-bar deadlifts and easier to learn.
commonMistakes: Looking down instead of keeping a neutral neck position.
Allowing the knees to cave inward during the pull.
Not keeping the bar close to the center of gravity.

name: Smith Machine Shrug
instructions: Stand inside a Smith machine with the bar at hip level.
Grip the bar shoulder-width or wider.
Shrug straight up, lifting your shoulders toward your ears.
Hold at the top, then lower.
The fixed bar path lets you focus on the contraction.
tips: Useful for training traps without worrying about bar path.
Can add more range by standing on plates.
description: A shrug performed inside a Smith machine with the fixed bar path. Useful for focusing on trap contraction without balancing the bar.
commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders instead of a straight vertical shrug.
Using excessive range only possible due to the fixed bar path.
Not pausing at the peak contraction.

name: Leg Press Calf Raise
instructions: Sit in a leg press machine and position just the balls of your feet on the bottom edge of the platform.
Allow the heels to drop below the platform level.
Press through the balls of your feet to extend your ankles.
Squeeze at the top, then lower with control.
Keep a slight bend in the knees throughout.
tips: Leg press calf raises allow very heavy loads.
Full range of motion is more important than heavy weight.
description: A calf raise performed on a leg press machine with just the balls of the feet. Allows very heavy loading with a full range of motion.
commonMistakes: Not lowering the heels below the platform for a full stretch.
Performing partial range of motion with heavy weight.
Locking the knees fully during the movement.

name: Jump Rope
instructions: Hold one handle of the rope in each hand.
Swing the rope over your head and jump as it passes under your feet.
Land on the balls of your feet.
Keep a consistent rhythm.
Progress to double-unders as skill improves.
tips: Excellent for calf endurance and cardiovascular conditioning.
Keep jumps low to the ground to reduce impact.
description: A rhythmic cardiovascular exercise jumping over a spinning rope. Excellent for calf conditioning, coordination, and cardiovascular fitness.
commonMistakes: Landing heavily on the heels rather than the balls of the feet.
Jumping too high above the rope, wasting energy.
Using arm movements that are too large, slowing rotation.

name: Box Jump
instructions: Stand in front of a sturdy box with feet shoulder-width apart.
Swing your arms back and hinge at the hips.
Jump explosively onto the box, landing softly with both feet.
Stand up fully on the box.
Step down (do not jump down to protect your joints).
tips: Start with a lower box and increase height progressively.
The landing should be soft — absorb with the ankles, knees, and hips.
description: An explosive plyometric exercise jumping onto an elevated box. Develops lower body power, rate of force development, and coordination.
commonMistakes: Jumping down from the box instead of stepping down, risking Achilles injury.
Landing with stiff legs instead of soft absorbing flexion.
Not landing with both feet simultaneously.

name: Walking Lunge
instructions: Stand with feet together, holding dumbbells at your sides (optional).
Step forward with one foot and lower the back knee toward the floor.
Push through the front heel to step forward into the next lunge.
Continue walking forward alternating legs.
Keep your torso upright throughout.
tips: A longer stride increases glute activation; shorter stride emphasizes quads.
Keep the front knee tracking over the toes.
description: A forward-moving lunge stepping continuously with alternating legs. Trains quads, glutes, and hamstrings across a full range of motion.
commonMistakes: Allowing the front knee to travel far past the toes.
Taking too small a step, creating excessive knee flexion.
Losing balance and rushing through the movement.

name: Sumo Deadlift
instructions: Stand with feet wider than shoulder-width, toes pointed out 30-45 degrees.
Grip the bar between your legs with arms straight.
Keep your chest up, brace your core, and push your knees out.
Drive through your legs to lift the bar.
Stand fully upright, squeezing glutes at the top.
tips: Sumo is more quad and inner thigh dominant than conventional.
Keeping the bar close to your body is crucial.
description: A deadlift variant with a wide stance and toes pointed outward. More quad and inner-thigh dominant than conventional with a shorter range of motion.
commonMistakes: Allowing the knees to cave inward instead of tracking over the toes.
Not maintaining a neutral lower back under heavy loads.
Letting the bar drift forward away from the body during the pull.

name: Nordic Hamstring Curl
instructions: Kneel on a pad with your ankles secured under a bar or held by a partner.
Slowly lower your body toward the floor by extending your knees.
Control the descent as long as possible.
Catch yourself with your hands when you can no longer resist.
Push off the floor to return to the kneeling position.
tips: One of the best exercises for hamstring injury prevention and strength.
Eccentric-only training initially — use push-up assist on the way up.
description: A bodyweight exercise controlling a slow fall from kneeling with ankles secured. Exceptionally effective for hamstring eccentric strength and injury prevention.
commonMistakes: Lowering too fast without controlling the eccentric phase.
Piking at the hips instead of maintaining a straight body line.
Attempting full reps before building sufficient eccentric strength.

name: Landmine Press
instructions: Place one end of a barbell in a landmine attachment or a corner.
Grip the other end with one or both hands at shoulder height.
Press the bar up and forward in an arc.
Lower with control back to shoulder height.
Keep your core braced and feet staggered for stability.
tips: The arc of the landmine press is shoulder-friendly for those with impingement issues.
Single-arm version also builds anti-rotation core stability.
description: A barbell press with one end anchored in a landmine attachment. The arc pattern is shoulder-friendly and works the anterior delt and upper chest.
commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to rotate significantly during unilateral pressing.
Not maintaining a stable core during the arc of the press.
Setting the landmine at an angle that is too shallow.

name: Push Press
instructions: Hold a barbell at shoulder level with a shoulder-width grip.
Slightly bend the knees (quarter squat).
Drive the bar overhead with a leg drive, then press with the shoulders.
Lock out the arms overhead.
Lower the bar back to shoulders under control.
tips: Push press lets you lift 15-20% more weight than strict overhead press.
The leg drive should initiate the bar movement, not the shoulders.
description: A shoulder press assisted by a leg drive to initiate the bar upward. Allows 15-20% heavier loads than strict pressing for overload stimulus.
commonMistakes: Dipping too deep, turning it into a full squat.
Not locking out the arms overhead at the top.
Pushing with the arms before the leg drive has transferred momentum.

name: Underhand Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at a lat pulldown machine with thighs under the pad.
Grip the bar at shoulder-width with an underhand (supinated) grip.
Pull the bar down to your upper chest.
Squeeze the lats at the bottom.
Slowly extend the arms back to the start.
tips: The underhand grip increases bicep involvement compared to overhand.
Lean back slightly (not excessively) for better lat activation.
description: A lat pulldown with an underhand (supinated) grip. Increases bicep involvement and some lifters find it easier to feel the lats working.
commonMistakes: Leaning back excessively and turning it into a row.
Not fully extending the arms at the top.
Pulling past the chin level to the chest.

name: Chest-Supported Row
instructions: Set a bench to a 45-degree incline and lie face down against it.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging.
Row the dumbbells up by driving elbows back.
Squeeze shoulder blades at the top.
Lower with control.
tips: Chest support eliminates body English and lower back involvement.
The incline position better targets the mid-traps and rhomboids.
description: A dumbbell row with the chest supported on an inclined bench. Eliminates lower back involvement for strict mid-back and rhomboid training.
commonMistakes: Not fully retracting the shoulder blades at the peak.
Allowing the chest to leave the pad during heavier reps.
Rowing to the upper chest instead of toward the hips.

name: Flat Dumbbell Bench Press
instructions: Lie flat on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand.
Hold the dumbbells at chest level with palms facing forward.
Press both dumbbells up until arms are fully extended.
Lower with control to chest level.
A greater range of motion is possible vs. barbell.
tips: Dumbbells allow each side to work independently, revealing imbalances.
Rotate wrists slightly inward at the top for a better contraction.
description: A flat bench press using dumbbells for independent arm movement. Allows a greater range of motion and reveals side-to-side strength imbalances.
commonMistakes: Allowing the dumbbells to drift too wide at the bottom.
Pressing the dumbbells straight up instead of together slightly.
Not controlling the eccentric with the extra range of motion.

name: Incline Cable Fly
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and grab a D-handle on each.
Lie on an incline bench between the cable stacks.
With arms slightly bent, bring the handles up and together above your chest.
Slowly open your arms back down.
Constant cable tension means the chest works throughout the full arc.
tips: Better than dumbbell flies for the upper chest due to constant tension.
Start light and focus on the squeeze.
description: A fly movement on an incline bench using low cable pulleys. Provides constant tension through the full arc for upper chest development.
commonMistakes: Bending the elbows too much and turning it into a cable press.
Not maintaining the slight elbow bend throughout.
Allowing the cables to pull the arms too far below parallel.

name: EZ-Bar Curl
instructions: Stand holding an EZ-bar with a shoulder-width underhand grip on the angled grips.
Keep your upper arms stationary at your sides.
Curl the bar up by contracting your biceps.
Squeeze at the top.
Lower with control.
tips: The EZ-bar reduces wrist strain compared to a straight bar.
The slight supination still targets the biceps effectively.
description: A bicep curl using an EZ-bar with angled grip positions. Reduces wrist strain compared to a straight bar while still effectively targeting the biceps.
commonMistakes: Still swinging the body to assist the curl.
Not reaching full extension at the bottom of each rep.
Gripping the wrong part of the EZ-bar, straining the wrists.

name: Overhead Cable Tricep Extension
instructions: Set a cable pulley to the highest position with a rope attachment.
Face away from the machine and grip the rope behind your head.
Stand in a staggered stance for stability.
Extend your arms overhead, pulling the rope forward.
Return to the starting position with control.
tips: Overhead position gives the long head of the tricep a full stretch.
Lean slightly forward to increase range of motion.
description: A cable tricep extension performed overhead facing away from the machine. The overhead position fully stretches the long head of the tricep.
commonMistakes: Letting the elbows flare out during the extension.
Not fully extending the arms at the end of the movement.
Leaning too far forward and losing core stability.

name: Crunch Machine
instructions: Adjust the machine so the pads rest on your chest or shoulders.
Grip the handles for stability.
Crunch forward by rounding your upper back.
Squeeze your abs at the peak of the contraction.
Return to the starting position with control.
tips: Allows progressive overload — increase weight weekly.
Exhale fully as you crunch for maximum contraction.
description: A machine-based crunch exercise allowing progressive loading. More consistent stimulus than bodyweight crunches due to added resistance.
commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing forward instead of controlled contraction.
Not going through the full range of spinal flexion.
Pulling with the arms instead of using abdominal muscles.

name: Windshield Wipers
instructions: Lie on your back with arms out to your sides.
Raise your legs to vertical.
Lower both legs to the right side without letting them touch the floor.
Return to vertical and lower to the left side.
Keep your shoulder blades on the floor.
tips: Advanced oblique and core rotation exercise.
Bend the knees for an easier version.
description: A supine rotational core exercise lowering legs side to side. An advanced oblique exercise requiring significant hip and core strength.
commonMistakes: Allowing the shoulder blades to leave the floor.
Lowering legs so far that they touch the ground, using momentum to return.
Holding breath during the movement.

name: Side Plank
instructions: Lie on your side with feet stacked and elbow directly under your shoulder.
Raise your hips off the ground, forming a straight line from head to feet.
Brace your core and hold for the target duration.
Switch sides.
Raise your top arm toward the ceiling for balance.
tips: Side planks specifically target the obliques and quadratus lumborum.
Add a hip dip (lower hip toward floor and back up) for extra challenge.
description: A lateral isometric hold on one forearm targeting the obliques. Essential for lateral core stability and oblique strength development.
commonMistakes: Allowing the hips to sag toward the floor.
Rotating the torso toward the floor during the hold.
Not maintaining a straight line from head to feet.

name: Cable Woodchop
instructions: Set the cable pulley to shoulder height on one side.
Stand sideways to the machine and grip the handle with both hands.
Rotate your torso away from the machine in a chopping motion.
Control the return rotation.
Repeat on both sides.
tips: The rotation should come from your core, not your arms.
Low-to-high and high-to-low variations target different oblique fibers.
description: A rotational cable exercise mimicking a chopping motion through the torso. Trains the obliques and rotational core strength functionally.
commonMistakes: Using the arms to pull rather than rotating from the core.
Planting the feet too close, reducing rotational stability.
Moving too quickly without controlled rotation on both directions.

name: Single-Leg Hip Thrust
instructions: Place upper back on a bench and extend one leg.
Drive through the heel of the grounded leg to lift your hips.
Squeeze glute at the top.
Lower with control.
Perform all reps on one side before switching.
tips: Single-leg version doubles load per glute.
Add a barbell across your hips for extra resistance.
description: A single-leg hip thrust with one leg extended, doubling the load per glute. Excellent for addressing glute imbalances and increasing intensity.
commonMistakes: Not fully extending the hip of the working leg at the top.
Pressing through the toes instead of the heel.
Allowing the pelvis to tilt rather than staying level.

name: Frog Pump
instructions: Lie on your back and place the soles of your feet together.
Allow your knees to fall out to the sides.
Push your hips up by squeezing your glutes.
Lower and repeat.
High reps (20-30) work well for this exercise.
tips: Great finisher exercise for glute pump.
Add a resistance band above the knees for more activation.
description: A floor-based glute exercise with feet together and knees flared out. Provides an excellent glute pump and is effective as a high-rep finisher.
commonMistakes: Not squeezing the glutes at the top of each rep.
Moving too fast and bouncing through reps.
Not keeping the soles of the feet firmly pressed together.

name: Seated Leg Curl
instructions: Sit in a seated leg curl machine with the pad across your shins.
Pull your heels toward your buttocks.
Squeeze hamstrings at the peak.
Slowly extend legs back to start.
Keep lower back pressed against the pad.
tips: Seated leg curl provides a stronger hamstring stretch than lying version.
Full range of motion is key — do not cheat with partial reps.
description: A seated machine exercise curling the legs against resistance. Provides a greater hamstring stretch than the lying leg curl variation.
commonMistakes: Not allowing full leg extension between reps.
Lifting the glutes off the seat during the curl.
Using momentum to swing the legs rather than controlled curling.

name: Pendlay Row
instructions: Stand over a barbell with feet hip-width apart.
Hinge to a near-horizontal torso position.
Pull the bar explosively to your lower chest.
Lower the bar completely to the floor between each rep.
Keep your back parallel to the floor throughout.
tips: The dead-stop variation removes momentum for a stricter movement.
The explosive pull trains power as well as strength.
description: A strict barbell row with the bar returned to the floor between each rep. The dead-stop removes momentum for a purer strength stimulus.
commonMistakes: Not returning the bar completely to the floor between reps.
Rounding the lower back due to a near-horizontal torso requirement.
Using a torso that is too upright, reducing the benefit over barbell rows.

name: Incline Hammer Curl
instructions: Set a bench to 45-60 degrees.
Sit back against the bench with dumbbells hanging in a neutral grip.
Curl the dumbbells up without rotating the wrists.
Squeeze the brachialis at the top.
Lower with full control.
tips: Incline position pre-stretches the brachialis for maximum activation.
Often used as a finisher after regular curls.
description: A hammer curl performed seated on an inclined bench. The pre-stretched brachialis position increases activation of this arm muscle.
commonMistakes: Not letting the arms hang completely straight at the start.
Swinging the upper arms forward during the curl.
Using too heavy a weight that eliminates the pre-stretch benefit.

name: Cable Face Pull with Rope
instructions: Set cable to forehead height with a rope attachment.
Grip both rope ends and step back.
Pull the rope to your forehead, separating hands at the end.
Elbows should be at or above shoulder height.
Hold briefly and return.
tips: Critical for shoulder health and balanced training.
Include this in every push day workout.
description: A face pull using a rope attachment at forehead height. Critical for rear deltoid development and shoulder external rotation health.
commonMistakes: Setting the cable too low so elbows drop below shoulder level.
Not separating the rope ends at the peak contraction.
Pulling the weight rather than focusing on external rotation.

name: Close-Grip Lat Pulldown
instructions: Sit at a pulldown machine with a V-handle or close-grip bar.
Lean back slightly and pull the handle to your upper chest.
Squeeze your lats at the bottom.
Slowly return to full arm extension.
Close grip emphasizes lower lats.
tips: The close grip allows a greater range of elbow travel, increasing lat activation.
Use a neutral grip handle for wrist comfort.
description: A lat pulldown with a close neutral grip attachment. Emphasizes the lower lats with an increased elbow travel range.
commonMistakes: Leaning back too far and turning it into a cable row.
Not fully extending the arms between reps.
Gripping the handle asymmetrically.

name: Smith Machine Squat
instructions: Position the bar on your upper back inside a Smith machine.
Stand with feet slightly forward of the bar.
Unlock the bar and descend until thighs are parallel.
Drive back up to standing.
Feet forward placement allows more upright torso.
tips: Good for beginners learning the squat pattern safely.
Place feet further forward to increase quad activation.
description: A squat performed inside a Smith machine with a fixed bar path. Useful for beginners learning the squat pattern or isolating quads.
commonMistakes: Placing feet too far forward and sitting back excessively.
Not achieving full depth due to the fixed bar path.
Over-relying on the Smith machine and neglecting free squat development.

name: Kettlebell Swing
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width, holding a kettlebell with both hands.
Hinge at the hips and let the kettlebell swing back between your legs.
Drive the hips forward explosively to swing the kettlebell to shoulder height.
Let gravity bring the kettlebell back and repeat the hip hinge.
This is a hip hinge, not a squat.
tips: Power comes from the hip drive, not the arms.
Keep the lats engaged throughout to protect the lower back.
description: A ballistic hip-hinge exercise swinging a kettlebell to shoulder height. Develops explosive posterior chain power and cardiovascular conditioning.
commonMistakes: Squatting the movement instead of performing a hip hinge.
Allowing the lower back to round during the hinge.
Using the arms to raise the kettlebell instead of hip drive.

name: Band Pull-Apart
instructions: Hold a resistance band in front of you at shoulder height.
Grip the band at shoulder-width.
Pull the band apart by moving arms out to your sides.
Squeeze your shoulder blades and rear delts.
Return slowly to the starting position.
tips: Excellent warm-up exercise and shoulder health movement.
High reps (20-30) are ideal for this exercise.
