description: A shoulder health exercise pulling a resistance band apart at chest level. Excellent warm-up for the rear delts and scapular retractors.
commonMistakes: Using a band with too much resistance that limits range.
Not achieving full arm extension behind the body at the end.
Performing the movement too quickly without controlling the return.

name: Reverse Hyperextension
instructions: Lie face down on a reverse hyper machine with hips at the edge.
Hold the handles for support.
Swing your legs up behind you by extending at the hips.
Squeeze glutes at the top.
Lower with control — the pendulum motion decompresses the spine.
tips: The reverse hyper is excellent for glute/hamstring development and lower back recovery.
Use added weight in the strap for progressive overload.
description: A machine exercise extending the legs behind the body while lying prone. Builds glutes and hamstrings while decompressing the lumbar spine.
commonMistakes: Hyperextending the lower back by swinging the legs too high.
Not controlling the pendulum return and losing tension.
Bending the knees excessively during the movement.

name: Incline Barbell Press
description: A compound pressing exercise on an inclined bench with a barbell. Targets the upper chest (clavicular pectorals) more directly than flat pressing.
instructions: Set a bench to a 30-45 degree incline and rack a barbell at the appropriate height.
Lie back on the bench and grip the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar and lower it to your upper chest with control.
Press the bar back up until arms are fully extended.
Keep your shoulder blades retracted and feet flat on the floor throughout.
tips: A 30-degree incline is more effective than 45 degrees for upper chest activation.
Keep your wrists straight and directly above your elbows throughout the press.
commonMistakes: Setting the bench angle too steep (above 45 degrees), shifting emphasis to front delts.
Flaring the elbows to 90 degrees, which stresses the shoulder joint.
Bouncing the bar off the chest instead of controlling the descent.

name: Incline Dumbbell Press
description: A compound pressing exercise on an inclined bench using dumbbells. Emphasizes the upper chest while allowing independent arm movement and a greater range of motion.
instructions: Set a bench to a 30-45 degree incline.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at chest level, palms facing forward.
Press the dumbbells up and slightly together until arms are fully extended.
Lower them slowly back to the starting position with control.
Keep your shoulder blades pinched together throughout the set.
tips: A 30-degree incline focuses more on the upper chest than 45 degrees.
Avoid letting the dumbbells drift too wide at the bottom of the movement.
commonMistakes: Setting the bench too steep, turning it into a shoulder press.
Allowing the dumbbells to drift too wide at the bottom.
Losing scapular retraction midway through the set.

name: Dumbbell Fly
description: A chest isolation exercise lying flat on a bench with dumbbells moving through a wide arc. Provides a deep chest stretch and strong peak contraction.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand directly over your chest.
With a slight bend in the elbows, lower the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc.
Feel the stretch across your pectorals at the bottom.
Squeeze the chest to bring the dumbbells back together over your chest.
Maintain the slight elbow bend throughout — do not straighten or over-bend.
tips: Think of hugging a large barrel to get the correct arc movement.
Keep the elbow angle consistent throughout — do not let it change.
commonMistakes: Bending the elbows too much, turning the movement into a dumbbell press.
Lowering the dumbbells too far and straining the shoulder joint.
Using too much weight that prevents a controlled range of motion.

name: Cable Crossover
description: A chest isolation exercise using high cable pulleys, bringing the hands together in front of the body. Provides constant tension and an excellent peak contraction.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the highest position and attach D-handles.
Stand in the center of the cable machine, one foot slightly forward for stability.
With a slight bend in the elbows, bring both handles down and together in front of your hips.
Cross one hand over the other at the bottom for full contraction.
Slowly return to the starting position feeling the chest stretch.
tips: The angle of the cable determines which part of the chest is targeted most.
Do not let the weight stacks touch between reps to maintain constant tension.
commonMistakes: Straightening the elbows and turning it into a pressing movement.
Allowing the weight stacks to touch and losing muscle tension.
Leaning too far forward and losing a stable base.

name: Push-Up
description: A classic bodyweight pressing exercise performed on the floor. Develops the chest, triceps, and shoulders while engaging the core as a stabilizer.
instructions: Start in a high plank position with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Keep your body in a straight line from head to heels.
Lower your chest toward the floor by bending your elbows.
Push through your palms to return to the starting position.
Keep your core braced and hips level throughout the movement.
tips: For more chest activation, use a wider hand position.
Elevate your feet to increase difficulty and upper chest emphasis.
commonMistakes: Letting the hips sag, turning the movement into a back-bending exercise.
Only performing partial reps without lowering the chest to the floor.
Placing hands too wide and flaring the elbows to 90 degrees.

name: Dumbbell Row
description: A unilateral back exercise rowing a dumbbell with one hand braced on a bench. Allows a full range of motion and targets the lats and mid-back independently.
instructions: Place one knee and hand on a bench for support, body parallel to the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in the opposite hand with arm fully extended.
Pull the dumbbell toward your hip by driving your elbow back.
Squeeze the lat and rhomboids at the top of the movement.
Lower the dumbbell with control to full arm extension.
tips: Allow the shoulder blade to fully protract at the bottom for a complete range of motion.
Think of pulling with your elbow, not your hand, to maximize lat engagement.
commonMistakes: Rotating the hips to use momentum rather than isolating the back.
Not allowing the shoulder blade to fully protract at the bottom.
Pulling the dumbbell upward rather than back toward the hip.

name: Dumbbell Pullover
description: A lying exercise lowering a dumbbell in an arc behind the head. Uniquely engages both the chest and lats depending on elbow position and torso angle.
instructions: Lie perpendicular to a bench with your upper back supported and hips low.
Hold a single dumbbell with both hands directly over your chest.
Lower the dumbbell in an arc behind your head with elbows slightly bent.
Feel the deep stretch across your chest and lats at the bottom.
Pull the dumbbell back through the same arc to the starting position.
tips: Keep your hips low to maximize the stretch across the ribcage.
Slightly bent elbows reduce elbow stress while maintaining chest/lat tension.
commonMistakes: Bending the elbows too much, reducing the stretch and making it a tricep exercise.
Raising the hips during the movement, reducing the effectiveness.
Using too much weight before mastering the movement pattern.

name: Deadlift
description: The foundational barbell pulling movement lifting a loaded bar from the floor. Works the entire posterior chain and is one of the most effective strength exercises.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart and the bar over your mid-foot.
Hinge at the hips and grip the bar just outside your legs.
Take a deep breath, brace your core hard, and flatten your back.
Push the floor away with your legs while keeping the bar close to your shins.
Stand tall, driving hips forward, with shoulders and hips rising at the same rate.
tips: Think 'push the floor away' rather than 'pull the bar up' for better leg drive.
Keep the bar in contact with your shins and thighs throughout the entire lift.
commonMistakes: Rounding the lower back, especially on the initial pull from the floor.
Letting the bar drift forward away from the body during the pull.
Jerking the bar explosively from the floor instead of building steady tension.

name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
description: A seated or standing overhead pressing movement using dumbbells. Develops all three deltoid heads with independent arm movement revealing imbalances.
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support or stand with core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward.
Press both dumbbells overhead until arms are fully extended.
Lower with control back to shoulder height.
Keep your core tight and avoid arching the lower back.
tips: Seated with back support allows heavier loads and better safety.
Slightly angling the dumbbells inward at the top reduces shoulder impingement.
commonMistakes: Hyperextending the lower back instead of maintaining a neutral spine.
Not reaching full arm extension overhead.
Pressing the dumbbells straight up instead of slightly inward at the top.

name: Lateral Raise
description: An isolation exercise raising the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The primary and most direct exercise for developing the medial deltoid head.
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing inward.
With a slight bend at the elbows, raise both arms out to the sides.
Lift until your arms are parallel to the floor at shoulder height.
Hold briefly at the top then lower with control.
Lead with your pinky fingers to maximize medial deltoid activation.
tips: Avoid using momentum by swinging your torso or shrugging your traps.
Slightly tilting the dumbbells pinky-side up increases lateral head activation.
commonMistakes: Swinging the body to generate momentum and lifting too heavy.
Raising the dumbbells above shoulder height unnecessarily, loading the traps.
Bending the elbows too much, reducing lateral deltoid tension.

name: Rear Delt Fly
description: A bent-over isolation exercise raising dumbbells laterally to target the posterior deltoids. Essential for shoulder balance and upper back health.
instructions: Hinge at the hips until your torso is near-parallel to the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms hanging down, slight bend in elbows.
Raise the dumbbells out to your sides keeping movement in the plane of your back.
Squeeze the rear delts and shoulder blades together at the top.
Lower the dumbbells with control back to the starting position.
tips: Keep the movement in the plane of your back, not in front of your torso.
Can be performed seated with chest resting on the thighs for strict isolation.
commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of maintaining a flat torso position.
Swinging the arms with momentum rather than controlled lateral raises.
Moving arms in front of the torso plane instead of directly to the side.

name: Dumbbell Curl
description: A bilateral or alternating curl using dumbbells, allowing wrist supination for a stronger peak contraction. A fundamental bicep isolation exercise.
instructions: Stand holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms at your sides.
With palms facing forward, curl both dumbbells simultaneously or alternate.
Supinate (rotate) your wrists outward as you curl for maximum contraction.
Pause at the top and squeeze the bicep.
Lower with control to full arm extension.
tips: Supinating the wrist as you curl produces a stronger bicep contraction.
Keep elbows pinned to your sides to prevent shoulder involvement.
commonMistakes: Swinging the torso to assist the curl with momentum.
Not supinating the wrists during the curl reducing contraction.
Allowing the elbow to drift forward at the top, reducing tension.

name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
description: A dumbbell curl performed on an inclined bench with arms hanging behind the torso. The pre-stretched position of the long head significantly increases bicep activation.
instructions: Set a bench to 45-60 degrees and sit back against it.
Let your arms hang straight down behind the body holding dumbbells.
Curl the dumbbells up while keeping your upper arms perpendicular to the floor.
Supinate the wrists at the top for maximum peak contraction.
Lower fully to maximize the long head stretch on every rep.
tips: The pre-stretched position of the long head significantly increases EMG activation.
Use lighter weight than standing curls due to the mechanical disadvantage.
commonMistakes: Not allowing arms to hang completely straight at the start.
Using too heavy a weight that eliminates the pre-stretch benefit.
Swinging the upper arms forward away from vertical during the curl.

name: Close-Grip Bench Press
description: A barbell bench press with a shoulder-width grip to maximize tricep involvement. One of the best compound movements for overall tricep mass and strength.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench and grip the barbell at shoulder-width.
Unrack the bar and keep your elbows close to your body.
Lower the bar to your lower chest in a controlled manner.
Press the bar back up explosively until arms are fully extended.
Keep elbows tucked throughout — do not let them flare wide.
tips: Going too narrow (inside shoulder-width) strains the wrists — stay at shoulder-width.
This is one of the most effective compound tricep movements available.
commonMistakes: Gripping too narrow which strains the wrists unnecessarily.
Flaring the elbows out wide rather than keeping them tucked.
Letting the bar drift too high toward the clavicle during the press.

name: Back Squat
description: The foundational barbell squat with the bar resting on the upper or lower traps. The king of lower body exercises for quad, glute, and overall leg development.
instructions: Place the barbell on your upper traps (high bar) or rear delts (low bar).
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly pointed out.
Brace your core and begin the descent by pushing your knees out over your toes.
Lower until your thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up maintaining chest position.
tips: Keep your chest up and avoid caving the knees inward during the ascent.
Depth depends on your anatomy — squat as deep as possible with good form.
commonMistakes: Caving the knees inward (valgus collapse) during the descent or ascent.
Shifting onto the toes and losing heel contact with the floor.
Rounding the lower back at the bottom of the squat.

name: Front Squat
description: A barbell squat with the bar resting on the front deltoids, requiring a more upright torso. Places greater emphasis on the quads and core compared to the back squat.
instructions: Position the barbell in the front rack position on your front deltoids.
Keep your elbows high and parallel to the floor throughout the movement.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly out.
Descend by pushing your knees out, maintaining an upright torso.
Drive through your full foot to stand up, keeping elbows high.
tips: Keeping elbows high prevents the bar from rolling off the shoulders.
More ankle and thoracic mobility is required than for the back squat.
commonMistakes: Dropping the elbows which causes the bar to roll forward off the shoulders.
Leaning forward excessively due to lack of thoracic or ankle mobility.
Not squatting to adequate depth due to mobility restrictions.

name: Lunge
description: A unilateral lower body exercise stepping forward and lowering the back knee toward the floor. Develops quads, glutes, and balance simultaneously.
instructions: Stand upright with feet together.
Step one foot forward and lower the back knee toward the floor.
Keep the front shin vertical and the torso upright.
Push through the front heel to return to the starting position.
Repeat on the opposite leg or perform all reps on one side.
tips: A longer stride increases glute activation; a shorter stride emphasizes quads.
Hold dumbbells or add a barbell for progressive overload.
commonMistakes: Allowing the front knee to travel far past the toes.
Taking too small a step, creating excessive knee flexion.
Leaning the torso forward instead of remaining upright.

name: Hip Thrust
description: A barbell hip extension exercise with the upper back on a bench and hips driving upward. Considered the most effective exercise for glute hypertrophy through hip extension.
instructions: Sit on the floor with your upper back against a bench and a barbell across your hips.
Plant your feet flat on the floor, shoulder-width apart.
Drive your hips upward by squeezing your glutes hard.
Hold at the top for one second with full hip extension.
Lower with control back toward the floor.
tips: Use a barbell pad for comfort under the barbell across the hips.
Push through your heels rather than your toes for better glute activation.
commonMistakes: Not achieving full hip extension at the top of the movement.
Letting the hips drop without controlling the descent.
Placing the barbell too high on the abdomen rather than on the hips.

name: Goblet Squat
description: A front-loaded squat holding a dumbbell or kettlebell at chest level. An excellent beginner squat variation that promotes an upright torso and good depth.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell or kettlebell vertically at chest height with both hands.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly pointed out.
Keeping the weight close to your chest, descend into a squat.
Lower until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your full foot to stand back up.
tips: The front-loaded position naturally encourages an upright torso.
Great for teaching squat mechanics before progressing to barbell squats.
commonMistakes: Letting the knees cave inward during the squat.
Leaning forward and dropping the weight from the chest position.
Using insufficient depth and missing the squat's training benefits.

name: Leg Raise
description: A supine core exercise raising the legs from the floor to vertical. Trains the lower abdominals and hip flexors through hip flexion.
instructions: Lie flat on your back with legs straight and hands under your lower back for support.
Keep your legs straight and raise them toward the ceiling.
Lift until your legs are perpendicular to the floor.
Slowly lower your legs back down without letting them touch the floor.
Keep your lower back pressed to the floor throughout.
tips: Press your lower back into the floor to ensure abs are doing the work.
Bend the knees slightly to reduce difficulty if needed.
commonMistakes: Allowing the lower back to arch off the floor as legs lower.
Using momentum to swing the legs up rather than controlled lifting.
Lowering legs completely to the floor and losing core tension.
